title: Demand and Supply
summary: The law of demand and supply, demand and supply curves, market equilibrium, and the variables that shift demand and supply.
content_html:
Demand and Supply

The interaction of demand and supply in markets determines prices and quantities. Understanding this is foundational to all business and economic analysis.

The Demand Side

Quantity demanded: The amount of a good a consumer is willing and able to buy at a given price.

Law of Demand: Holding everything else constant (ceteris paribus), when price falls, quantity demanded increases; when price rises, quantity demanded decreases. The demand curve is downward-sloping.

Two explanations for the law of demand:

  • Substitution effect: When a good becomes more expensive, consumers switch to cheaper substitutes.
  • Income effect: A price increase reduces consumer purchasing power, so they buy less.

Variables that Shift Demand

  1. Income: Normal goods -- demand rises with income. Inferior goods -- demand falls as income rises.
  2. Prices of related goods: Substitutes (can replace each other), Complements (used together).
  3. Tastes
  4. Population and demographics
  5. Expected future prices

The Supply Side

Law of Supply: Holding everything else constant, when price rises, quantity supplied increases; when price falls, quantity supplied decreases. The supply curve is upward-sloping.

Variables that shift supply: input prices, technology, expected future prices, number of sellers.

Market Equilibrium

Market equilibrium occurs where quantity demanded equals quantity supplied -- the intersection of the demand and supply curves. At equilibrium, there is no shortage (excess demand) or surplus (excess supply).

  • Shortage: Price is below equilibrium -- quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied. Price rises to restore equilibrium.
  • Surplus: Price is above equilibrium -- quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded. Price falls to restore equilibrium.

exercises.title: Law of demand
exercises.prompt: The law of demand states that, ceteris paribus:
options: As price rises, quantity demanded rises
As price falls, quantity demanded falls
As price rises, quantity demanded falls
Price and quantity demanded are unrelated
exercises.answer: As price rises, quantity demanded falls

exercises.title: Normal good
exercises.prompt: A normal good is one for which:
options: Demand increases as income falls
Demand is unaffected by income changes
Demand increases as income rises
Supply increases as price falls

exercises.title: Inferior good
exercises.prompt: An inferior good is one for which:
options: Demand increases as income rises
Demand decreases as income falls
Demand increases as income falls
Supply decreases as price rises

exercises.title: Substitutes
options: Are used together (e.g. coffee and sugar)
Can be used for the same or similar purpose
Are always more expensive than alternatives
Are produced using the same inputs
exercises.answer: Can be used for the same or similar purpose

exercises.title: Market equilibrium
options: Price is set by the government
Quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied
Quantity demanded equals quantity supplied
Supply exceeds demand at every price

exercises.title: Shortage cause
exercises.prompt: A shortage in a market occurs when:
options: Price is above equilibrium
Quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded
Price is below equilibrium, causing excess demand
The supply curve shifts right
exercises.answer: Price is below equilibrium, causing excess demand

exercises.title: Ceteris paribus
exercises.prompt: The term ceteris paribus in economics means:
options: Everything changes simultaneously
All else being equal -- other variables held constant
Prices always rise over time
Supply determines demand
exercises.answer: All else being equal -- other variables held constant

exercises.title: Demand shift vs movement
exercises.prompt: A change in the price of a good causes:
options: A shift in the demand curve
A movement ALONG the existing demand curve
A shift in both demand and supply curves
No change in quantity demanded
exercises.answer: A movement ALONG the existing demand curve
